summary: Gruber publishes his grades for Apple's 2020 performance across twelve categories as part of Jason Snell's annual Six Colors Report Card. He awards top marks to Mac (driven by the M1 revolution), iPhone, and iPad, while giving Apple TV hardware a D for neglect and Developer Relations a C for App Store overreach. His most notable revision is downgrading Apple Software Quality from B to C in hindsight, citing a design team that prioritizes aesthetics over usability. The overall picture is of a company firing on most cylinders but with significant blind spots in TV strategy, developer goodwill, and interaction design philosophy.
key_points: The M1 Macs represent the best moment in Mac hardware history, earning an easy A grade
iPad's addition of full mouse and keyboard support was the single biggest iPad improvement in memory, fundamentally changing what tasks the device is good for
Apple TV hardware gets a D — Apple clearly cares about TV content but has no coherent hardware strategy, with no update since 2017
Gruber retroactively downgrades software quality, arguing Apple's HI team under Alan Dye prioritizes how things look over how they work
Developer relations suffer because Apple squeezes the App Store too hard, driving developers toward cross-platform strategies that ultimately hurt Apple's ecosystem
money_quotes: The M1 Macs mark the best moment in Mac hardware history. Apple Silicon is that big a deal.
You can see the bones of a conceptual system that will last for decades, just like MacOS has.
This is the single biggest addition to iPad in memory — it completely changes the number of tasks and sort of apps iPad is 'good for'.
Selling a would-be game box without a gaming controller probably isn't a good strategy.
But you can't chalk it up to 'Well Apple just doesn't care about TV' because the success of Apple TV+ and continuing excellence of the iTunes Store show they clearly do.
There's a real sense that Apple's current HI team, under Alan Dye, is a 'design is what it looks like' group, not a 'design is how it works' group.

It's not in Apple's interests to squeeze every last cent out of the App Store. They've caught the attention of government regulators around the world, and developers, rightly or wrongly, feel like they're being squeezed.
The power dynamics of the App Store are such that developers feel like they need to do the opposite: to build cross-platform apps and systems to avoid relying on Apple.
key_insight: Apple's 2020 was defined by the triumphant M1 transition and iPad maturation, but undermined by a design philosophy that prizes appearance over function and App Store policies that alienate the developer community Apple depends on.
